rounds.answer_a:
The courts that review federal trial-court decisions are the U.S. courts of appeals, or circuit courts. They sit between the district courts and the Supreme Court and, in practice, are the final decision-makers in most federal cases because the Supreme Court accepts only a tiny fraction of petitions for review.[1][2] There are 13 appellate courts: 12 regional circuits plus the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit.[1] Their jurisdiction is primarily appellate, reviewing alleged legal error in district-court decisions and appeals from many federal administrative agencies.[1][3] Cases are usually decided by three-judge panels, although some matters are reheard en banc by all active judges of a circuit or a larger authorized group.[1][3] They do not retry facts or hear new evidence; instead, they review the record and written and oral arguments and issue opinions that affirm, reverse, remand, or modify lower-court rulings.[1][3] Thousands of appeals are filed annually, far more than the Supreme Court can hear, so appellate judgments ordinarily end the case.[2][3]

Federal probation and pretrial services officers use the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model to guide supervision aimed at reducing recidivism.[4][5] The main tools are the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for people under post-conviction supervision and the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) for release and detention decisions before trial.[4][6] Under RNR, officers concentrate resources on higher-risk individuals, target criminogenic needs linked to reoffending, and match interventions to each person’s abilities, motivation, and circumstances.[4][5] This framework promotes evidence-based practices such as differential supervision intensity, case planning, incentives and sanctions, cognitive-behavioral interventions, and ongoing reassessment.[4][5]

## References
1. United States Courts. “Courts of Appeals.” https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure/courts-appeals
2. Supreme Court of the United States. “Guide for Prospective Indigent Petitioners for Writs of Certiorari.” https://www.supremecourt.gov/casehand/guideforIFPcases2019.pdf
3. Federal Judicial Center. “The U.S. Courts of Appeals and the Federal Judiciary.” https://www.fjc.gov/history/courts/u-s-courts-appeals-and-federal-judiciary
4. United States Courts, Office of Probation and Pretrial Services. “Overview of Probation and Pretrial Services Supervision.” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision
5. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Risk-Need-Responsivity in Federal Probation and Pretrial Services.” https://www.uscourts.gov
6. United States Courts. “Post Conviction Risk Assessment and Pretrial Risk Assessment.” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services
rounds.answer_b:
The court that reviews trial-level decisions and, in most instances, provides the last word in federal litigation is the U.S. Courts of Appeals. Thirteen circuits (12 regional circuits plus the nationwide Federal Circuit) sit below the Supreme Court and above 94 district courts [1]. Each appeal is ordinarily decided by a three-judge panel; the full circuit may rehear a case en banc, but this is rare. The courts possess mandatory jurisdiction over final judgments and certain interlocutory orders from district courts, as well as specified agency rulings [1][2]. Roughly 50,000 appeals are filed annually, yet the Supreme Court reviews fewer than 1 percent of them, so about 99 percent of appellate decisions are effectively final for the parties [2]. Written opinions—published or unpublished—create binding precedent within the circuit, contributing to nationwide uniformity unless conflicts prompt Supreme Court review.

Federal probation and pretrial services officers supervise defendants and persons on release under an evidence-based framework grounded in the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model [3]. At the bail stage, officers administer the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) to gauge likelihood of failure to appear or re-offending; after conviction they use the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) to measure criminogenic risk and needs, recalibrating it over time [3][4]. Core RNR principles guide resource allocation: 1) concentrate the greatest dosage of supervision and services on higher-risk individuals (risk principle), 2) target dynamic criminogenic needs such as substance abuse or antisocial attitudes (need principle), and 3) match interventions to learning style, motivation, culture, and gender (responsivity principle). Officers employ structured skill-building, cognitive-behavioral strategies, and graduated incentives/sanctions to reduce recidivism while maximizing public safety [4].

## References
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Understanding the Federal Courts: The U.S. Courts of Appeals.” uscourts.gov, 2023.  
2. Federal Judicial Center. “Caseloads in the U.S. Courts of Appeals.” fjc.gov, 2022 statistics.  
3. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Federal Probation and Pretrial Services: Charter for Excellence & EBP Framework.” uscourts.gov, 2021.  
4. La Touche, P. & Hanson, R. “Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) 2.1: Validation and Implementation.” Federal Probation Journal, Vol. 81(2), 2017.

On WIDTH, both cover nearly all checklist items (structure, 12 regional + Federal Circuit, three-judge panels, no new evidence, finality due to limited Supreme Court review, PTRA/PCRA tools, RNR principles, and core correctional practices). rounds.answer_a:
The two recurring judiciary-wide statistical publications that fit your criteria are **Judicial Business of the United States Courts** and the **Federal Court Management Statistics (FCMS)** reports.[1][2]

**Judicial Business** is the federal judiciary’s principal **annual** statistical compendium. It covers a **12-month period ending September 30** of each fiscal year.[1] Its coverage is broad and nationwide, including the **U.S. courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and the probation and pretrial services system**, with tables on filings, terminations, pending cases, workload trends, and other administrative measures.[1][3] Publicly posted editions currently run from **1997 through 2024** on the U.S. Courts website.[1]

**Federal Court Management Statistics** is also issued on a recurring basis, but it is organized around **two different 12-month windows each year**, producing reports for periods ending **March 31** and **September 30**.[2] The judiciary describes FCMS as being published **biannually**.[2] Its scope is likewise nationwide and multi-court, with statistics for the **courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and probation/pretrial services**, but the emphasis is management-style workload snapshots such as pending cases per judgeship, filings, weighted filings, median times, and related court performance indicators.[2][4] Publicly available FCMS reports extend back much farther than Judicial Business, with archives on the U.S. Courts site reaching from **March 31, 1985, through September 30, 2024**.[2]

The key difference is therefore both **timing** and **content emphasis**. **Judicial Business** follows the judiciary’s standard **fiscal-year cycle** and functions as the comprehensive annual narrative-and-table report on national court business.[1][3] **FCMS**, by contrast, provides **twice-yearly management statistics** anchored to two reporting end dates—**March 31 and September 30**—and is more focused on comparative operational indicators useful for court administration and workload monitoring rather than the fuller annual statistical narrative.[2][4]

## References
1. U.S. Courts, “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/judicial-business-united-states-courts  
2. U.S. Courts, “Federal Court Management Statistics.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/federal-court-management-statistics  
3. U.S. Courts, “Judicial Business 2024.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/judicial-business-2024  
4. U.S. Courts, “Federal Court Management Statistics Profiles.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/federal-court-management-statistics-profiles
rounds.answer_b:
Two nationwide statistical series meet the criteria.  

1. Judicial Business of the United States Courts  
• Reporting window: the federal fiscal year – 12-month period ending September 30.  
• Publication cadence: issued once each fiscal year, usually the following March.  
• Coverage: workload data for every Article III level (courts of appeals, district courts, and the Court of International Trade) plus Article I bankruptcy courts, magistrate judges, federal probation and pretrial services, and related matters such as civil and criminal case terminations, juror usage, and wiretap activity.  
• Publicly available span: AO-USCourts has posted complete volumes from FY 1995 to FY 2023; selected tables go back to FY 1970.  
2. Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics (formerly Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary)  
• Reporting window: the statistical year – 12-month period ending March 31.  
• Publication cadence: released annually, typically in June.  
• Coverage: consolidated national tables for the U.S. courts of appeals, district courts (civil and criminal), bankruptcy courts, and magistrate judges.  It tracks filings, terminations, and pending totals but excludes probation, pretrial, and ancillary administrative topics.  
• Publicly available span: tables are posted for each statistical year from 1997 through 2023; some earlier spreadsheets date to 1990.

Contrast.  Although both series aggregate the same core adjudicatory units, they partition time differently—Judicial Business follows the federal government’s fiscal calendar, while Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics closes its books six months earlier on March 31.  Judicial Business is a narrative-plus-tables report that pairs statistics with explanatory text and includes supervision services and specialty topics, whereas FJCS is a tables-only compendium focused strictly on case counts and dispositions.  Consequently, researchers gain two staggered snapshots each year: preliminary figures (FJCS) three months after the March close and fuller, policy-oriented figures (Judicial Business) about six months after the fiscal year ends.  

## References  
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business 2023.” uscourts.gov, Mar. 2024.  
2. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics 2023.” uscourts.gov, Jun. 2023.  
3. AO-USCourts Archives. Historical Judicial Business volumes, 1995-2022.  
4. AO-USCourts Archives. Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary, 1997-2022.

The courts directly below the Supreme Court are the U.S. courts of appeals, usually called circuit courts. They are the federal appellate courts that review district-court proceedings to determine whether the trial was conducted fairly and whether the law was interpreted and applied correctly.[1][2] There are 13 circuits in all: 12 regional circuits that group the district courts geographically, plus the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which has specialized nationwide jurisdiction.[1] Appeals are usually decided by three-judge panels, though some cases are reheard en banc by all active judges of a circuit or a larger authorized subset.[1][2] Their jurisdiction is primarily appellate, including review of final decisions from district courts and many federal administrative agencies.[1][2] Because the Supreme Court hears only a very small percentage of petitions for certiorari, courts of appeals are the final decision-makers in the overwhelming majority of federal cases.[3]

Federal probation and pretrial services officers use an evidence-based supervision framework grounded in the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model to reduce recidivism.[4][5] Its core principles are to match supervision intensity to risk level, target criminogenic needs that drive reoffending, and tailor interventions to an individual’s characteristics, abilities, and motivation.[4][5] The primary tools are the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) for pretrial release/detention decisions and supervision, and the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for people under probation, supervised release, or other post-conviction supervision.[4][6] The framework emphasizes structured assessment, case planning, motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral approaches, incentives and sanctions, reassessment, and directing officer time and treatment resources toward higher-risk individuals most likely to benefit.[4][5]

rounds.answer_b:
The courts directly beneath the Supreme Court are the U.S. Courts of Appeals. Ninety-four district courts are clustered into 12 regional circuits, plus the nationwide Federal Circuit, making 13 appellate courts in all [1]. Appeals are ordinarily heard by rotating three-judge panels; a full “en banc” rehearing by all active judges in the circuit occurs only in exceptional situations [1]. The courts have mandatory jurisdiction over final judgments from district courts and specified administrative agencies, reviewing whether trial fact-finding was legally sufficient and whether the correct law was applied [2]. Roughly 50,000 appeals are filed each year, yet the Supreme Court grants certiorari in fewer than 1 percent, so about 99 percent of circuit decisions represent the parties’ last word and set binding precedent within the circuit [3].

Federal probation and pretrial services supervision is anchored in an evidence-based framework built on the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model [4]. Three principles drive practice: (1) Risk—concentrate controls and services on higher-risk persons; (2) Need—target dynamic criminogenic factors such as antisocial peers or substance abuse; (3) Responsivity—match interventions to learning style, motivation, culture, and gender [4]. Officers employ two actuarial tools: the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) at the bail stage, forecasting failure to appear and new criminal conduct, and the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for probation, supervised release, and parole, which is recalibrated throughout supervision to track change [5]. Core skills include motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral coaching, problem-solving techniques, and the calibrated use of incentives and graduated sanctions—practices shown to lower recidivism while safeguarding communities [4][5].

## References
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “The U.S. Courts of Appeals.” uscourts.gov, 2023.  
2. Federal Judicial Center. “Jurisdiction of the Courts of Appeals.” fjc.gov, 2022.  
3. Supreme Court Statistics. “Caseload Overview 2022 Term.” supremecourt.gov, 2023.  
4. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Probation and Pretrial Services Evidence-Based Practices Implementation Guide.” 2021.  
5. La Touche, P. & Hanson, R. “Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) 2.1 Validation.” Federal Probation, 81(2), 2017.

rounds.answer_a:
The two recurring statistical publications are **Judicial Business of the United States Courts** and the **Federal Court Management Statistics (FCMS)** reports.[1][2]

**Judicial Business** is the judiciary’s **annual** statistical publication and is the principal year-end workload report for the federal courts.[1] Its scope is broad: it covers the **Supreme Court, courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, probation and pretrial services, and judicial compensation**.[1] For appellate workload, it reports filings, terminations, pending cases, procedural categories, and historical comparisons, often with tables showing changes from earlier years.[1] It uses a **12-month period ending September 30** of the named fiscal year.[1] Its online archive extends back to **1997**.[1]

**Federal Court Management Statistics** is issued **twice each year** and is designed as a compact source of **frequently requested caseload information** for the federal judiciary.[2] Its scope covers the principal adjudicative units of the federal courts, including **courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and the pretrial services system**; it presents court-by-court management tables rather than the broader narrative yearbook format of Judicial Business.[2] FCMS uses two rolling **12-month periods** each year: one ending **March 31** and one ending **September 30**.[2] The reports include comparative indicators such as **filings, terminations, pending cases, median times, per-judge statistics, and percentage change from the same period in the previous year**, allowing quick workload comparisons across courts and over time.[2]

In short, **Judicial Business** is the judiciary’s comprehensive annual statistical yearbook, while **FCMS** is the semiannual, table-driven management series for quick caseload comparison.[1][2] The public online archives for FCMS extend back to **September 2004**, while Judicial Business reaches back to **1997**.[1][2]

## References
1. U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/judicial-business-united-states-courts  
2. U.S. Courts. “Federal Court Management Statistics.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/federal-court-management-statistics
rounds.answer_b:
The two recurring statistical series are:

1. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts” (the statistical section of the Director’s Annual Report).  
   (1) Scope: Comprehensive fiscal-year statistics for every Article III component—the 13 courts of appeals, 94 district courts (civil, criminal, and magistrate matters), and all bankruptcy courts; it also summarizes pretrial/probation activity and federal defender work.  
   (2) Reporting period: the 12-month federal fiscal year, 1 October-30 September.  
   (3) Data: new filings, terminations, pending caseloads, median disposition times, appeals by origin and subject, trial days, weighted filings per judgeship, long-term trend tables, and narrative analysis comparing the current year with the prior year and with decade-earlier levels.  
   (4) Public archive: PDFs and tables are posted back to fiscal year 1997 on uscourts.gov, giving more than 25 years of continuous data [1].

2. “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary” (semi-annual caseload tables).  
   (1) Scope: Frequently requested tables covering the same three court tiers—courts of appeals, district courts, and bankruptcy courts—plus limited figures for magistrate judges.  
   (2) Reporting period: a rolling 12-month span ending 31 March (first edition) and 30 September (second edition), thereby updating caseloads twice each year.  
   (3) Data: about 40 standardized tables showing filings, terminations, and pending matters by case category (e.g., criminal offense type, civil nature-of-suit code, bankruptcy chapter), comparative percent changes from the previous 12-month period, and per-judgeship workload indicators for appellate and district courts.  
   (4) Public archive: searchable PDFs extend back to March 1998, providing more than two decades of semi-annual trend data [2].

Together, these publications give policymakers, researchers, and the public regularly updated snapshots (semi-annual) and in-depth fiscal-year analyses (annual) of appellate, district, and bankruptcy court workloads.

## References  
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts, Fiscal Year 2023” (and prior years), uscourts.gov.  
2. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary, 12-Month Period Ending March 31, 2023” (and archive back to 1998), uscourts.gov.

rounds.answer_a:
The federal judiciary’s main recurring workload-reporting series are centered on different 12-month periods. **Judicial Business of the United States Courts** is the judiciary’s comprehensive **annual** statistical yearbook, covering the Supreme Court, courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, probation and pretrial services, and related judiciary functions; it reports by the **federal fiscal year ending September 30**.[1] **Federal Court Management Statistics (FCMS)** is a compact, table-driven series of frequently requested caseload statistics for courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and pretrial services; it is issued for 12-month periods ending **March 31** and **September 30**.[2] The **Caseload Statistics Data Tables** provide judiciary-wide statistical tables, including appellate and district court filings, terminations, pending cases, and related indicators, and are published for 12-month periods ending **June 30**, **September 30**, and **December 31**.[3]

Together, these publications show how the judiciary tracks workload across different reporting cycles: FCMS supports midyear and year-end management comparisons; Judicial Business serves as the official annual statistical synthesis; and the Data Tables provide rolling updates for additional quarter endpoints.[1][2][3]

For the U.S. Courts of Appeals specifically, the current judiciary vacancy page reports **179 congressionally authorized judgeships** and **10 vacancies**.[4] That authorized-judgeship figure refers to active appellate judgeships created by Congress across the regional circuits and the Federal Circuit.[4]

Official judiciary guidance is also published through handbooks and manuals. Examples include the **Judges’ Journalists Guide**, which explains court structure, procedure, terminology, and media-coverage practices for reporters covering federal courts.[5] The **Court Reporter Management Plan Guide to Judiciary Policy** guides courts and administrators on managing official court reporting services, transcripts, procurement, and compliance with judiciary policy.[6] The **Federal Court Interpreter Orientation and Terms of Service Manual** provides interpreters and court personnel with policy, ethics, orientation, and service standards for interpreter work in federal courts.[7] Another example is the **Chambers Handbook for Judges’ Law Clerks and Court Staff Attorneys**, which outlines roles, ethics, confidentiality, workflow, and professional expectations for chambers personnel.[8] Collectively, these resources complement the statistical series by documenting not only caseload and structure, but also the operational rules and professional standards that support the federal judiciary.[5][6][7][8]

rounds.answer_b:
The federal judiciary uses several recurring statistical series—each keyed to a different 12-month span—to give Congress, policymakers, and the public regular snapshots of appellate, district, and bankruptcy activity.

1. Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary – 12-month periods ending 31 March and 30 September. Issued twice a year, this set of about forty tables supplies frequently requested counts of filings, terminations, and pending cases for the courts of appeals, district courts, and bankruptcy courts, with percent-change comparisons to the prior year [1].

2. Judicial Business of the United States Courts – fiscal year ending 30 September. Published in the Director’s Annual Report, it delivers narrative analysis plus detailed tables on filings, trial days, median disposition times, and workload per judgeship across all Article III courts and the bankruptcy system [2].

3. Federal Court Management Statistics – 12-month period ending 30 June. Updated each fall, these two-page profiles for every court of appeals and district court present five-year trends in weighted filings, median time from filing to disposition, case terminations per three-judge panel, and authorized judgeships, permitting mid-fiscal-year benchmarking [3].

4. Judicial Facts and Figures – calendar year ending 31 December. Released annually, this compilation aggregates long-term trend tables (some stretching back to 1945) on appeals by subject matter, district civil and criminal filings, prisoner petitions, bankruptcy filings, and federal defender caseloads, allowing comparisons independent of the fiscal calendar [4].

Congressional authorization and vacancies (as of the latest public posting): 179 judgeships for the U.S. Courts of Appeals, with 25 vacancies awaiting nomination or confirmation [5].

Key judiciary handbooks and manuals that support consistent administration:

• Guide to Judiciary Policy – a multi-volume manual issued by the Administrative Office covering ethics, case management, procurement, information technology, and defender services; it serves as the primary administrative rulebook for judges and court unit executives [6].

• Judicial Writing Manual – prepared by the Federal Judicial Center to help appellate and district judges craft clear, well-reasoned opinions, with style conventions, citation guidance, and strategies for managing complex records [7].

• Journalist’s Guide to the Federal Courts – an AO-produced primer that explains court structure, terminology, electronic access rules, and courtroom protocol, enabling reporters to cover proceedings accurately and without disrupting operations [8].

These statistical series and reference manuals together illuminate how the judiciary monitors its workload, allocates resources, and disseminates practical guidance for those who run, or report on, the nation’s appellate courts.

## References  
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts (AO). “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary, 12-Month Period Ending March 31, 2024.”  
2. AO. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts, Fiscal Year 2023.”  
3. AO. “Federal Court Management Statistics, 2023 Release (12-Month Period Ending June 30, 2023).”  
4. AO. “Judicial Facts and Figures, Table B-1 to B-9 (Calendar Year 2023).”  
5. U.S. Courts. “Judicial Vacancies—U.S. Courts of Appeals” (accessed April 2026).  
6. AO. “Guide to Judiciary Policy” (current edition).  
7. Federal Judicial Center. “Judicial Writing Manual: A Pocket Guide for Judges,” 3d ed., 2022.  
8. AO. “Journalist’s Guide to the Federal Courts,” 2020 update.

rounds.answer_a:
The annual report series is **Judicial Business of the United States Courts**, the federal judiciary’s comprehensive statistical yearbook. It reports on a **12-month period ending September 30** and covers the workload of the federal courts broadly, including the **Supreme Court, the courts of appeals, the district courts, the bankruptcy courts, probation and pretrial services, and judicial compensation**.[1] On the U.S. Courts website, the publicly available editions run continuously from **1997 through 2024**, covering the late 1990s through the mid-2020s.[1] The report includes tables and analysis on matters such as filings, terminations, pending cases, case types, historical comparisons, and other judiciary-wide workload indicators.[1]

In the **U.S. courts of appeals**, cases are ordinarily decided by a **three-judge panel** rather than by all judges of the circuit.[2][3] These courts review the trial-court record to determine whether proceedings were fair and whether the law was applied correctly; they do not retry the case or hear new evidence.[2] If oral argument is held, each side is typically given a **short, fixed amount of time—often about 10 to 15 minutes per side, and commonly around 15 minutes in federal appellate practice**—to highlight the key legal issues and answer judges’ questions rather than present evidence.[3][4]

After the court of appeals issues its decision, a case may be reviewed by the **Supreme Court of the United States** if that Court grants a **petition for writ of certiorari**, but the Supreme Court accepts only a very small share of petitions, so most appellate rulings are effectively final.[2][5] A case also may be reheard **en banc**, meaning before **all active judges of the circuit or, in some circuits, a larger authorized panel**, when the court concludes that such rehearing is needed to maintain uniformity of the circuit’s decisions or because the proceeding involves a question of exceptional importance.[2][6]

## References
1. U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/judicial-business-united-states-courts  
2. U.S. Courts. “Courts of Appeals.” https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure/courts-appeals  
3. Federal Judicial Center. “The U.S. Courts of Appeals and the Federal Judiciary.” https://www.fjc.gov/history/courts/u-s-courts-appeals-and-federal-judiciary  
4. United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. “Frequently Asked Questions.” https://www.ca9.uscourts.gov/mediation/frequently-asked-questions/  
5. Supreme Court of the United States. “Guide for Prospective Indigent Petitioners for Writs of Certiorari.” https://www.supremecourt.gov/casehand/guideforIFPcases2019.pdf  
6. Legal Information Institute, Cornell Law School. “Rule 35. En Banc Determination.” https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frap/rule_35
rounds.answer_b:
“Judicial Business of the United States Courts” is the federal judiciary’s official annual statistical series.  Issued by the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts each fiscal year (12-month period ending September 30), it aggregates workload data for every Article III component—13 courts of appeals, 94 district courts (civil, criminal, and magistrate matters), and all bankruptcy courts—and also reports on probation, pretrial services, federal defenders, and authorized judgeships [1].  The on-line archive supplies continuous volumes from FY 1997 through FY 2023, giving researchers a publicly available run of 27 editions spanning the late 1990s to the mid-2020s [1][2].

Within each volume tables and narrative compare filings, terminations, and pending cases for:
• Courts of appeals (filings by origin, subject matter, and pro se status).  
• District courts—civil (nature-of-suit, motions, trials) and criminal (offense type, defendants, trial days).  
• Bankruptcy courts (chapters, adversary proceedings).  
• Probation and pretrial services (persons under supervision, revocations).  
• Defender organizations and wiretap authorizations.  
Trend charts show multi-year changes, median times to disposition, and weighted filings per judgeship, enabling workload and resource analysis across the entire federal system [1].

Handling of appeals:  
1. Panel composition – Appeals are ordinarily decided by a rotating three-judge panel of circuit judges; one of them is designated to write the opinion [3].  
2. Oral argument – After briefs, most cases submitted for argument receive 10–20 minutes per side (frequently 15) to answer judges’ questions and clarify disputed legal points; many uncomplicated cases are resolved without argument on the written record alone [3].  
3. Further review – A party may petition the full circuit for rehearing en banc; such rehearing is granted only by a majority vote of active judges when needed to secure intra-circuit uniformity or address a question of exceptional importance (Fed. R. App. P. 35) [4].  Beyond that, a discretionary writ of certiorari may be sought from the U.S. Supreme Court, which accepts fewer than 1 percent of petitions, making most court-of-appeals decisions final for the litigants and binding within the circuit [5].

## References  
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts, Fiscal Year 2023.” uscourts.gov, 2024.  
2. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. Judicial Business archive (FY 1997–present). uscourts.gov.  
3. Federal Judicial Center. “Structure of the Federal Courts: Courts of Appeals.” fjc.gov, 2023.  
4. Federal Rules of Appellate Procedure 35, “En Banc Determination.” 28 U.S.C. app.  
5. Supreme Court of the United States. “The Court and Its Procedures – Certiorari Statistics.” supremecourt.gov, 2023.

**Judicial Business of the United States Courts** is the judiciary’s comprehensive **annual** statistical yearbook. It covers the **12-month fiscal year ending September 30**.[1] Its scope is broad, analyzing workload for the **Supreme Court, courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, probation and pretrial services, and judicial compensation**.[1] The public online archive currently runs from **1997 through 2024**.[1]

**Federal Court Management Statistics (FCMS)** is issued **semiannually** and presents frequently requested caseload tables for federal court management.[2] It reports two rolling **12-month periods**, one ending **March 31** and one ending **September 30**, with separate releases for each cycle.[2] FCMS covers the **courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and the pretrial services system**, supplying court-by-court comparative measures such as filings, terminations, pending cases, and other management indicators.[2] Its online archive extends back to **September 2004**.[2]

**Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics** is another **annual** judiciary-wide statistical publication, but unlike Judicial Business it covers a **12-month period ending March 31**.[3] It reports workload for the **courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and federal probation and pretrial services**.[3] The publication emphasizes comparative caseload tables and trend snapshots across these court units and functions.[3] Its publicly available archive extends back to **March 2008**.[3]

Appeals from federal trial courts are handled by the **U.S. courts of appeals**, which review the district court record to determine whether proceedings were fair and whether the law was correctly applied.[4] Appeals are usually decided by **three-judge panels**, not by all judges of a circuit.[4][5] In limited circumstances, a case may be reheard **en banc** by all active circuit judges, or by a larger authorized panel, typically to secure uniformity of decisions or address questions of exceptional importance.[5] A further appeal to the **U.S. Supreme Court** is possible only if that Court grants **certiorari**, which it does in only a small fraction of cases, making most appellate rulings effectively final.[4][6]

## References
1. U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/judicial-business-united-states-courts  
2. U.S. Courts. “Federal Court Management Statistics.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/federal-court-management-statistics  
3. U.S. Courts. “Federal Judicial Caseload Statistics.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/federal-judicial-caseload-statistics  
4. U.S. Courts. “Courts of Appeals.” https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure/courts-appeals  
5. Legal Information Institute, Cornell Law School. “Rule 35. En Banc Determination.” https://www.law.cornell.edu/rules/frap/rule_35  
6. Supreme Court of the United States. “Guide for Prospective Indigent Petitioners for Writs of Certiorari.” https://www.supremecourt.gov/casehand/guideforIFPcases2019.pdf
rounds.answer_b:
The federal judiciary releases three principal, regularly issued statistical series, each built on a different 12-month reporting cycle.

1. Judicial Business of the United States Courts  
   1) Annual fiscal-year report.  
   2) Covers the 12-month period 1 October-30 September; issued once each year.  
   3) Provides system-wide workload data for the 13 courts of appeals, 94 district courts, all bankruptcy courts, and for federal probation, pretrial services, defenders, and wiretaps.  
   4) PDF archives are publicly posted back to FY 1997 (27 editions) [1].

2. Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary  
   1) Semi-annual caseload tables.  
   2) Each edition summarizes a rolling 12-month span ending 31 March (spring set) or 30 September (fall set); published twice per year.  
   3) Presents about forty frequently requested tables for courts of appeals, district courts, bankruptcy courts, and limited magistrate-judge activity.  
   4) Online archive reaches back to the March 1998 cycle (more than 50 semi-annual releases) [2].

3. Federal Court Management Statistics (FCMS)  
   1) Annual court-management series.  
   2) Uses the 12-month period ending 30 June; issued once yearly.  
   3) Supplies per-court profiles—filings, terminations, pending cases, median disposition times, and judgeship-adjusted workload—for every court of appeals and district court, plus comparable national summaries and selected bankruptcy indicators.  
   4) Downloadable PDFs and spreadsheets are available back to FY 1998 for circuit courts and 2001 for district courts [3].

Appeals processing in the U.S. Courts of Appeals  
• Panel structure: Appeals are ordinarily assigned to a rotating three-judge panel; one judge authors the opinion, and the panel’s decision constitutes precedent within that circuit.  
• Oral argument: After written briefs, argued cases typically receive 10–20 minutes per side, allowing judges to probe legal errors and clarify facts; many less complex appeals are decided on the briefs alone.  
• Further review: A party may seek (a) rehearing en banc, granted by majority vote of the active circuit judges only when necessary for intra-circuit uniformity or issues of exceptional importance (Fed. R. App. P. 35), or (b) discretionary certiorari from the U.S. Supreme Court. Because the Court accepts under 1 percent of petitions, roughly 99 percent of appellate rulings remain the final word for the litigants [4][5].

## References  
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts (AO) – Judicial Business archive, uscourts.gov.  
2. AO – Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary, 12-Month Period Ending March 31, 2024 (archive links back to 1998).  
3. AO – Federal Court Management Statistics, 2023 release (archive index).  
4. Federal Judicial Center – Structure & Jurisdiction of the U.S. Courts of Appeals, 2023.  
5. Supreme Court of the United States – Certiorari statistics, 2024.

rounds.answer_a:
The courts are the U.S. courts of appeals (the circuit courts), the federal tier directly below the Supreme Court. They primarily review district-court decisions for legal error, asking whether proceedings were fair and whether law was correctly interpreted and applied.[1][2] The system has 13 courts of appeals: 12 regional circuits that group district courts geographically and the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which has specialized nationwide jurisdiction.[1] Appeals are usually decided by three-judge panels; some cases are reheard en banc by all active circuit judges, or a larger authorized subset.[1][2] Their caseload is large—tens of thousands of filings annually—and because the Supreme Court grants review in only a small fraction of cases, circuit decisions are effectively final in most federal litigation.[1][3] Beyond ordinary criminal and civil trial appeals, they also review many decisions of federal administrative agencies and certain other specialized matters assigned by statute.[1][2]

The supervision function is federal probation and pretrial services, which supervises people released before trial and individuals serving probation, supervised release, parole in limited legacy cases, or other post-conviction terms.[4][5] Its research-driven framework is the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model.[4][6] RNR directs officers to match supervision intensity to an individual’s risk level, target criminogenic needs linked to reoffending, and tailor interventions to personal characteristics, abilities, and motivation.[4][6] The judiciary uses the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) at the pretrial stage and the Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) for post-conviction supervision.[6][7] Officers emphasize evidence-based practices such as structured assessment, case planning, motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral interventions, graduated incentives and sanctions, referral to treatment and services, and ongoing reassessment to encourage behavioral change and reduce recidivism.[4][6]

## References
1. U.S. Courts. “Courts of Appeals.” https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure/courts-appeals  
2. Federal Judicial Center. “The U.S. Courts of Appeals and the Federal Judiciary.” https://www.fjc.gov/history/courts/u-s-courts-appeals-and-federal-judiciary  
3. Supreme Court of the United States. “Guide for Prospective Indigent Petitioners for Writs of Certiorari.” https://www.supremecourt.gov/casehand/guideforIFPcases2019.pdf  
4. U.S. Courts. “Overview of Probation and Pretrial Services Supervision.” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/supervision  
5. U.S. Courts. “Overview of Probation and Pretrial Services.” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/overview  
6. U.S. Courts. “Risk and Needs Assessment.” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services/risk-and-needs-assessment  
7. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) and Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA).” https://www.uscourts.gov/services-forms/probation-and-pretrial-services
rounds.answer_b:
The federal courts that sit directly beneath the Supreme Court are the U.S. Courts of Appeals. Ninety-four district courts are clustered into 12 regional circuits plus the District of Columbia Circuit, while a separate Federal Circuit hears nationwide subject-matter appeals, giving 13 appellate courts in all [1]. Each case is ordinarily resolved by a rotating three-judge panel; all active judges may rehear an exceptional matter en banc [1]. Their mandatory jurisdiction covers final judgments and many interlocutory orders from district courts, reviews of federal administrative agencies, some bankruptcy appeals, and certain petitions such as habeas corpus or immigration matters [2]. About 48,000 appeals are filed yearly, yet the Supreme Court grants certiorari in well under 1 percent, so roughly 99 percent of circuit decisions are the parties’ last word and set binding precedent within that circuit [3].

The federal supervision function is carried out by U.S. Probation and Pretrial Services. Its work is grounded in the Risk-Need-Responsivity (RNR) model, an evidence-based framework showing that recidivism falls when (1) supervision intensity matches an individual’s risk level, (2) interventions target dynamic criminogenic needs, and (3) services are delivered in a style and setting people can absorb [4]. Officers apply two actuarial instruments: the Pretrial Risk Assessment (PTRA) to predict failure-to-appear or new criminal conduct before trial, and the Post-Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) to gauge risk and needs throughout probation, supervised release, or parole [4][5]. Guided by assessment results, officers employ motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral coaching, problem-solving skill building, and a structured system of incentives and graduated sanctions—practices empirically linked to behavioral change [5]. This research-driven approach enables the judiciary to allocate resources where they have the greatest public-safety payoff while supporting lawful, pro-social outcomes for people under supervision.

## References
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Understanding the Federal Courts: Courts of Appeals.” uscourts.gov, 2023.  
2. Federal Judicial Center. “Jurisdiction and Procedure of the U.S. Courts of Appeals.” fjc.gov, 2022.  
3. Supreme Court of the United States. “Statistics for the 2022 Term.” supremecourt.gov, 2023.  
4. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts. “Probation and Pretrial Services: Evidence-Based Practices Implementation Guide.” 2021.  
5. Hanson, R. & La Touche, P. “Post Conviction Risk Assessment (PCRA) 2.1 Validation.” Federal Probation, Vol. 81(2), 2017.

rounds.answer_a:
The courts are the U.S. courts of appeals, the federal appellate tier directly below the Supreme Court. A losing party in a federal trial court ordinarily seeks review by filing a notice of appeal, after which the appeal is handled mostly through the written record rather than a new trial. The parties submit written briefs explaining the facts, legal issues, and claimed errors in the district court’s decision; the appellate court reviews that record to decide whether the proceedings were fair and whether the law was correctly applied.[1][2]

Cases are usually decided by panels of three judges rather than by a single judge or a jury.[1] In many appeals, the court also hears oral argument, typically giving each side a short, fixed time—commonly about 10 to 15 minutes—to answer judges’ questions and highlight key points from the briefs.[2][3] After argument, the judges confer and issue a written decision. If a party seeks further review within the same circuit, it may ask for rehearing en banc, in which a larger group of judges—usually all active circuit judges, or in the Ninth Circuit a limited en banc court—reconsiders the panel’s ruling.[1][2] The Supreme Court may then become involved if a party files a petition for a writ of certiorari, but review is discretionary and granted in only a very small percentage of cases, so courts of appeals are effectively the final decision-makers in most federal litigation.[1][4]

Two official statistical publications regularly track appellate workload. First, the **Judicial Business of the United States Courts** is issued annually and reports data for the 12-month period ending September 30 of each year.[5] It provides detailed comparative statistics on filings, terminations, pending cases, procedural categories, and median times, allowing comparisons among circuits and over time.[5] Second, the **Federal Court Management Statistics** are issued quarterly and present rolling 12-month data ending March 31, June 30, September 30, and December 31.[6] For courts of appeals, they provide circuit-by-circuit management and workload measures such as filings, terminations, pending appeals, and age or processing indicators useful for comparative performance assessment.[6]

## References
1. U.S. Courts. “Courts of Appeals.” https://www.uscourts.gov/about-federal-courts/court-role-and-structure/courts-appeals  
2. Federal Judicial Center. “The U.S. Courts of Appeals and the Federal Judiciary.” https://www.fjc.gov/history/courts/u-s-courts-appeals-and-federal-judiciary  
3. United States Court of Appeals for the Fourth Circuit. “Oral Argument.” https://www.ca4.uscourts.gov/oral-argument/oral-argument-information  
4. Supreme Court of the United States. “Guide for Prospective Indigent Petitioners for Writs of Certiorari.” https://www.supremecourt.gov/casehand/guideforIFPcases2019.pdf  
5. U.S. Courts. “Judicial Business of the United States Courts.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/analysis-reports/judicial-business-united-states-courts  
6. U.S. Courts. “Federal Court Management Statistics.” https://www.uscourts.gov/statistics-reports/federal-court-management-statistics
rounds.answer_b:
A litigant who loses in a U.S. district court ordinarily begins review by filing a notice of appeal in the district court within 30 days; the clerk transmits the record to the U.S. court of appeals for the geographic (or, for certain subject-matter cases, the Federal) circuit [1].  

1. Written briefs.  The appellant files an opening brief explaining alleged legal errors; the appellee responds; and the appellant may reply. Briefs must conform to Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 28 and are the primary vehicle for persuasion—many appeals are decided on the briefs alone.  

2. Oral argument.  When the court believes additional clarification would help, it schedules oral argument. Under FRAP 34(d) the default allotment is 15 minutes per side, though panels may shorten or lengthen that time [2]. Argument is strictly timed and focuses on questions from the bench.  

3. Panel composition.  Each case is normally assigned to a randomly selected panel of three Article III judges drawn from the circuit’s active judges and, occasionally, senior judges or visiting judges from other circuits [1]. The panel issues a precedential or non-precedential opinion and judgment.  

4. En banc review.  A party dissatisfied with the panel’s decision may petition for rehearing en banc. If a majority of the circuit’s active judges vote to grant, either all active judges (or, in the Ninth Circuit, an 11-judge limited en banc panel) rehear the case; such rehearings are granted sparingly to maintain decisional uniformity or address questions of exceptional importance [3].  

5. Supreme Court review.  After final circuit action, a party may petition the Supreme Court for a writ of certiorari. The Court grants roughly 70–80 of the 7,000–8,000 petitions filed each term, so fewer than 1 percent of court-of-appeals decisions receive further review, making the circuit courts the court of last resort in most federal cases [4].  

Official workload statistics for the courts of appeals are reported in two recurring publications:  

• Judicial Business of the United States Courts.  Issued once a year, it covers the 12-month fiscal year ending September 30. It presents narrative analysis and detailed tables comparing filings, terminations, pending cases, median disposition times, and appeals by subject matter across circuits and over time [5].  

• Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary.  Released quarterly, each set covers the rolling 12-month period ending March 31, June 30, September 30, or December 31. Tables B-1 through B-12 provide circuit-by-circuit counts of appeals filed, terminated, pending, method of disposition, and reversal rates, enabling users to track short-term workload trends and make inter-circuit comparisons [6].  

## References  
1. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, “Understanding the Federal Courts: U.S. Courts of Appeals,” uscourts.gov, 2023.  
2. Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 34(d), 2023 ed.  
3. 28 U.S.C. § 46(c); Ninth Cir. R. 35-3.  
4. Supreme Court of the United States, “Statistics for the 2022 Term,” supremecourt.gov, 2023.  
5. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, “Judicial Business 2023,” uscourts.gov, published March 2024.  
6. Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts, “Statistical Tables for the Federal Judiciary—Quarter Ending June 30 2023,” uscourts.gov.
